<!-- Generated by the logos-redteam multi-agent workflow, 2026-06-21.
     68 agents, ~2.0M tokens, 23 confirmed findings. Internal AI red-team —
     NOT a substitute for a professional third-party cryptographic audit. -->

# Logos Red-Team Report — Final Consolidated Findings

## Executive Summary

Logos is an experimental Rust + SwiftUI end-to-end-encrypted messenger built on the
"Sealed" blueprint (Double Ratchet, PQXDH hybrid handshake, sealed-sender metadata,
TOFU identity pinning, an untrusted axum relay). This review red-teamed the workspace
against the stated threat model (untrusted relay; malicious peers; at-rest device
protection; goals of confidentiality/integrity/authenticity, forward secrecy,
post-compromise security, PQ-hybrid confidentiality, classical sealed-sender metadata,
no key/nonce reuse, and relay-abuse/DoS resistance).

**Overall the codebase is in good shape.** The cryptographic core is sound: no
message confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, key-reuse, or impersonation break
survived adversarial verification. The Double Ratchet's transactional decrypt holds,
TOFU pinning and the prekey-path identity binding are enforced, and the prior-review
fixes (mailbox key-confusion, atomic store save, transactional ratchet, last-resort
single-use guards, etc.) all held up under re-examination.

The substantive surviving risk falls into two buckets:

1. **Relay DoS / resource exhaustion (the only HIGH items).** The relay persists its
   entire state on nearly every request and lacks global admission control, so an
   unauthenticated attacker can drive quadratic disk/CPU amplification and unbounded
   memory/disk growth.
2. **Forward-secrecy erosion over time (MEDIUM).** One-time prekeys are drainable by
   anyone, are never replenished by the client, and the signed prekey never rotates —
   so every conversation eventually and permanently downgrades to the reusable
   last-resort KEM with no one-time X25519 leg, defeating harvest-now-decrypt-later
   resistance for the first message of each conversation.

The rest are an iOS identity-teardown race, a narrow `reset_session` replay window, an
unbounded quarantine DoS, and a cluster of LOW hardening/error-handling/zeroization
items. Several originally-higher findings were downgraded by verification to
defense-in-depth (non-exploitable under the stated threat model) and are recorded as
INFO.

## Severity Summary

| # | Severity | Finding | Exploitable | Area |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|------|
| H1 | **High** | Per-request full-state snapshot → O(N²) DoS amplification | Yes | relay |
| H2 | **High** | No global caps / per-IP limits → unauthenticated state-exhaustion DoS | Yes | relay |
| M1 | **Medium** | Permanent FS downgrade via one-time-prekey exhaustion (no replenish / no rotation / no downgrade detection) | Yes | pqxdh + client + relay |
| M2 | **Medium** | `startNewIdentity()` race resurrects "deleted" identity + history on disk | Yes | ios / recovery |
| M3 | **Medium** | `reset_session` re-opens a prekey replay/reset window | Yes (narrow) | client |
| M4 | **Medium** | Unbounded, never-aged quarantine → peer can permanently fill a victim's mailbox | Yes | client + relay |
| L1 | Low | Quadratic skipped-key eviction (`Vec::remove(0)`) → peer CPU amplification | Yes | ratchet |
| L2 | Low | Sealed-sender metadata has no forward secrecy | Yes (cond.) | sealed-sender |
| L3 | Low | Unzeroized transient copies of the long-term identity secret | No (hygiene) | identity / client |
| L4 | Low | `StoreKey.swift` ignores Security-framework return codes (RNG + Keychain) | No | ios at-rest |
| L5 | Low | `save()` failure silently drops the just-decrypted batch | No | client |
| L6 | Low | Snapshot has no fsync + corrupt snapshot is fatal on boot | No (availability) | relay |
| L7 | Low | CI installs build tools unpinned (cargo-audit, xcodegen) | Yes (supply-chain) | ci |
| I1 | Info | Non-strict ed25519 verify + no low-order X25519 validation (hardening) | No | crypto-primitives |
| I2 | Info | Normal path doesn't cross-check `cert.sender_identity` vs pinned peer | No | sealed-sender |

---

## Findings (detail)

### H1 — Per-request full-state snapshot causes O(N²) DoS amplification

- **Location:** `crates/logos-server/src/lib.rs:233-253` (`save_snapshot`), called at
  `:370` (register), `:397` (directory **read**), `:466` (post_mailbox), `:514` (ack),
  `:538` (replenish).
- **Impact:** Every mutating request — and the *unauthenticated* `GET
  /v1/directory/{username}` read path — clones the entire `mailboxes` map under the
  single global mutex (`inner.mailboxes.clone()`, `:245`) and then synchronously
  serializes and rewrites the whole snapshot file (blocking a tokio worker; no
  `spawn_blocking`). Cost is O(total stored state) per request. As state grows to N,
  total work becomes O(N²), and the O(N) clone is held under the lock that serializes
  all handlers.
- **Attack:** An attacker inflates state (see H2), after which every request from
  anyone — including legitimate fetch/ack — pays a full-state clone + multi-MB/GB disk
  rewrite. Even absent prior inflation, a single attacker hammering unauthenticated
  `GET /v1/directory/<known-user>` forces a full-state disk write per read (the
  endpoint "mutates" by popping a prekey, but writes a snapshot even when nothing
  pops). Throughput collapses.
- **Note from verification:** only the O(N) clone is under the global lock; the
  serialize+write happens after the lock is dropped (still synchronous/blocking). This
  narrows the lock-hold but does not change the verdict — confirmed, exploitable, High.
- **Fix:** Do not snapshot synchronously per request. Mark state dirty and let the
  existing 60s maintenance task be the sole writer, or debounce/coalesce writes. Never
  snapshot from a read path (directory). Avoid cloning the whole map under the lock;
  move to incremental/per-key persistence (e.g. redb, as the module doc anticipates)
  and run writes on `spawn_blocking`.

### H2 — No global resource caps / per-IP rate limiting → unauthenticated state-exhaustion DoS

This is one admission-control root cause manifesting on three endpoints (originally
filed as three relay-abuse findings; merged here).

- **Locations:**
  - Mailbox flood: `crates/logos-server/src/lib.rs:434-468` (`post_mailbox`,
    unauthenticated), rate key is the caller-chosen path param
    `RateKey::MailboxId(id.clone())` (`:446`); only per-mailbox cap
    `MAX_MAILBOX_MESSAGES` at `:453`.
  - Registration flood: `:338-372` (`register`) — no rate limit, no directory-size cap;
    the signature only proves control of an attacker-generated key.
  - Replenish flood: `:519-540` (`replenish`) — `entry.one_time.extend(...)` /
    `entry.kem_one_time.extend(...)` at `:535-536` with no pool-size cap.
- **Impact:** No cap on number of mailboxes, number of directory entries, or aggregate
  stored bytes, and the only rate limiter (`RateKey` enum has a single `MailboxId`
  variant) is keyed on an attacker-controlled value. There is no per-IP limiting.
- **Attack:**
  1. POST to `/v1/mailbox/<fresh-random-id>` each request: both the token bucket
     (starts at burst=100 per new id) and the 4096-message cap are keyed per id, so a
     new id always passes. With 1 MiB bodies, RAM and on-disk snapshot grow unbounded;
     entries persist for the 7-day TTL.
  2. Loop `register` with fresh self-signed keypairs: directory `HashMap` grows without
     bound (never pruned) and squats the username namespace.
  3. (Authenticated, lower-tier) loop `replenish` to grow one account's prekey
     `VecDeque`s without bound.
  Each of these also triggers H1's full-state rewrite per request, compounding into
  quadratic disk amplification. Relay-abuse/DoS resistance is an explicit goal; the two
  primary vectors are unauthenticated.
- **Note:** The prior "single-lock rate-check in post_mailbox" fix closed a TOCTOU
  race, not this attacker-controlled-key bypass. The `RateLimiter` doc comment claiming
  a size-cap eviction is stale; buckets are only time-pruned after 1h.
- **Fix:** Add a global ceiling on total mailbox count, directory entries, and
  aggregate queued/prekey bytes; reject past the cap. Add per-IP rate limiting (plumb
  `ConnectInfo`) on unauthenticated paths instead of trusting a caller-chosen key.
  Consider restricting `post_mailbox` to ids backed by a registered directory entry,
  and cap per-account prekey pools at `replenish`.

### M1 — Permanent forward-secrecy downgrade via one-time-prekey exhaustion

Merged from three dimensions (pqxdh handshake, relay-abuse directory drain,
client-engine no-replenishment) — one root issue.

- **Locations:**
  - `crates/logos-pqxdh/src/lib.rs:94-149` (`initiate`): validates only
    `bundle.verify()`; silently omits the one-time X25519 leg (`dh4`) when absent and
    never checks `kem_prekey.id != 0` (last-resort). No downgrade flag in the result.
  - `crates/logos-identity/src/lib.rs:222-244` (`PreKeyBundle::verify`): the
    last-resort KEM key (id 0) is validly signed and indistinguishable from a one-time
    KEM prekey.
  - `crates/logos-server/src/lib.rs:374-399` (directory): unauthenticated, unrate-
    limited GET that `pop_front()`s a one-time X25519 prekey and a one-time KEM prekey,
    `unwrap_or_else(last_resort_kem.clone())`.
  - `crates/logos-client/src/lib.rs:406-443,473`: mints 20 OTK / 10 KEM-OTK / 1
    last-resort *once* at registration; `signed_prekey_id` hardcoded to 1 and never
    rotated; the client never calls `/v1/replenish` (the server endpoint is dead code).
- **Impact:** Anyone (untrusted relay, or any unauthenticated client via ~20 directory
  GETs) can drain both one-time pools, which never refill. From then on, every new
  handshake the victim accepts is derived solely from long-lived, never-deleted secrets
  — `IK_B`, the never-rotated `SPK_B`, and the reusable last-resort ML-KEM key — plus
  the public ephemeral `EK_A` that the relay harvests inside the sealed envelope it
  stores. A later one-time compromise of those long-term secrets lets the attacker
  recompute `dh1/dh2/dh3` and the KEM shared secret and reconstruct the root key for
  the first message of every conversation. This defeats the stated forward-secrecy and
  harvest-now-decrypt-later goals for initial messages. The Double Ratchet still heals
  subsequent messages. The last-resort fallback is by-design in PQXDH, but Signal makes
  it acceptable by rotating the signed prekey and replenishing one-time prekeys — Logos
  does neither, turning a transient, bounded reduction into a permanent total one, with
  no UI signal.
- **Fix:** (a) In `initiate()`, surface a downgrade flag / require caller opt-in when
  `bundle.one_time_prekey.is_none() || bundle.kem_prekey.id == 0`. (b) Client: when the
  local one-time pools fall below a low-water mark, mint fresh prekeys and POST to
  `/v1/replenish` (signed via `replenish_signed_bytes`); periodically rotate the signed
  prekey (new id, re-sign, publish, retire the old after in-flight drain). (c) Rate-
  limit / lightly authenticate directory GETs so the pool cannot be drained for free.

### M2 — `startNewIdentity()` races with in-flight engine calls, resurrecting the "deleted" identity and history

- **Location:** `ios/LogosApp/Sources/Session.swift:117-128` (`startNewIdentity`) with
  `:507-527` (`pollOnce`) and `crates/logos-client/src/lib.rs:705-753` (`recv` →
  `self.save()`); cross-relay variant at `Session.swift:98-111` (`switchRelay`).
- **Impact:** `Session` is `@MainActor`, but every Rust call hops to
  `DispatchQueue.global` via `runBlocking`, releasing the (reentrant) actor.
  `pollOnce()` captures a strong local ref to the old `LogosClient` and `await`s
  `recv()`, which advances the ratchet and ends with an unconditional `self.save()`
  (tmp-write + rename to the captured path). While that network+save is in flight, the
  user can invoke `startNewIdentity()`, which cancels the cooperative poll task, sets
  `self.client=nil`, and `removeItem()`s the store/history/avatar files. The captured
  client is not freed, so its in-flight `save()` re-creates the deleted store
  containing the OLD `identity_secret` + `identity_seed` (the full recovery seed) after
  deletion. When `pollOnce` resumes it never re-validates identity, appends the old
  identity's decrypted plaintext into `messages`/`conversations`, and `persist()`s,
  re-creating the deleted history file. The `StoreKey` Keychain entry is never rotated,
  so the resurrected files remain fully decryptable.
- **Attack:** A user who runs `startNewIdentity()` to destroy a coerced/compromised
  identity, with a poll or send in flight, silently re-persists the recovery seed and
  plaintext history to disk after the "delete." A later device acquisition (at-rest is
  in scope) recovers the abandoned identity's keys and history and can impersonate the
  identity forever (no PCS for the seed). The `switchRelay` variant writes one relay's
  conversations into another relay's local store.
- **Fix:** Serialize identity teardown with the engine: before unlinking files, await
  cancellation/completion of the poll task and any in-flight `runBlocking` (single-
  flight actor mutex / `await task.value`), then drop the client and delete. In
  `pollOnce()`, capture a generation/identity token at entry and bail after the `await`
  if `self.client` changed before mutating state or calling `persist()`. On
  `startNewIdentity()`, also delete and re-mint the `StoreKey` Keychain entry so any
  resurrected ciphertext is undecryptable.

### M3 — `reset_session` re-opens the prekey replay/reset window

- **Location:** `crates/logos-client/src/lib.rs:926-929` (`reset_session`) vs the
  no-clobber guard at `:808-810` and `establish_and_decrypt` `:931-1014`.
- **Impact:** The no-clobber guard refuses to let an inbound prekey message clobber a
  live session (to stop replayed initials resetting the ratchet). `reset_session`
  deletes the local session while keeping the TOFU pin, so the next inbound prekey is
  accepted again. `establish_and_decrypt` only re-checks the (unchanged) pinned
  identity and the (never-rotating) `signed_prekey_id`; it has no replay record of
  consumed initial messages. In the post-exhaustion last-resort state (see M1), a
  replay of a previously captured genuine initial envelope re-derives the identical
  root key and decrypts.
- **Attack:** After the user invokes `reset_session(peer)` ("your contact reinstalled;
  ask them to resend"), a malicious relay replays an OLD captured initial envelope from
  the still-pinned contact instead of forwarding the new handshake. It is accepted, the
  contact's OLD first message is redelivered as fresh, and the session is installed on
  the stale ratchet — after which the contact's genuine handshake is dropped by the
  no-clobber guard, silently locking the conversation on attacker-chosen replayed
  state. No plaintext disclosure, forgery, or impersonation (TOFU still binds the same
  contact); impact is stale-content redelivery + a stealthy session-lock DoS.
- **Verification note:** Severity split low/medium across verifiers. One verifier also
  observed the embedded sender cert carries `expires_unix` (24h TTL,
  `verify_certificate`), so the replay window is bounded to ~24h, not unlimited — this
  caps but does not close the issue.
- **Fix:** Give `establish_and_decrypt` replay protection independent of session
  existence: persist a small set of consumed initial-message identifiers (hash over
  `initiator_identity || kem_ciphertext || ephemeral_pub || ratchet header`) and reject
  duplicates even after `reset_session`. Alternatively, have `reset_session`
  rotate/replenish the responder's prekeys (and require the next handshake to consume a
  fresh one-time prekey) so a replayed initial can no longer re-derive the same root
  key.

### M4 — Unbounded, never-aged quarantine lets a peer permanently fill a victim's mailbox

- **Location:** `crates/logos-client/src/lib.rs:747` (`Err(_) => {}` leaves the
  envelope on the server) and `:814-829` (a `Normal` message with no session →
  `Err` → quarantine), with the per-fetch cap at `:722` (`MAX_FETCH_ENVELOPES`).
- **Impact:** A `Normal` envelope for which no session exists is quarantined (left on
  the server, never ACKed) on the assumption it arrived ahead of its prekey. There is
  no expiry, dedup, attempt-count, or per-sender/total cap. Unlike unseal-failures and
  live-session decrypt-failures (which return `Ok(None)` and are ACK-deleted, so the
  victim self-heals), quarantined envelopes are the one attacker-postable class the
  victim never drains.
- **Attack:** A malicious peer registers, obtains a valid sender cert, and posts up to
  `MAX_MAILBOX_MESSAGES` (4096) cheap `Normal` envelopes that will never have a session.
  The victim can never clear them, so subsequent posts from *any* sender hit
  `INSUFFICIENT_STORAGE` — a persistent, victim-unrecoverable inbound DoS, sustainable
  indefinitely by topping up as the 7-day TTL expires entries, plus ~1000 wasted
  re-unseals (X25519 DH + cert verify) per poll. (The relay-injected variant — forging
  a cert with the server signing key — is non-additive, since the relay can already
  drop/inject; the peer-vs-honest-relay variant is the stronger, in-scope path.)
- **Verification note:** Severity split low/medium; the mailbox-pinning DoS justifies
  Medium.
- **Fix:** Bound the quarantine: tag each quarantined id with first-seen
  timestamp/attempt count and ACK-drop after a small TTL or N attempts; cap total and
  per-sender quarantined ids; and use a server-side fetch cursor so stuck envelopes
  can't occupy the fetch window.

### L1 — Quadratic skipped-key eviction (`Vec::remove(0)`) enables peer CPU amplification

- **Location:** `crates/logos-ratchet/src/lib.rs:309-323` (eviction at `:314-316`).
- **Impact:** `skip_message_keys` evicts with `self.skipped.remove(0)` (O(n) memmove of
  68-byte entries) inside the skip loop. At the `MAX_SKIP_TOTAL`=2000 cap, a single
  message can drive up to `MAX_SKIP`=1000 iterations × ~2000-element shifts; `decrypt`
  calls it twice (pn + n) → ~272 MB of memmove worst case per message, plus a full-
  state clone (~136 KB) per decrypt. `dh_ratchet` does not clear `skipped`, so the
  table stays full and an attacker can reset `nr` via a chosen `header.dh` to sustain
  this.
- **Attack:** An established malicious peer (AEAD-valid messages required, so the relay
  alone can't trigger it — forged messages roll back) fills the skipped table, then
  streams small valid messages that each force ~1000 skips, burning tens of ms of
  memmove+HMAC per tens-of-bytes message — a ~10× amplification that pins a CPU
  core/battery. Bounded: no memory exhaustion, no confidentiality/integrity impact,
  single session only.
- **Fix:** Use a `VecDeque` with O(1) `pop_front()`, or batch-evict with a single
  `drain(0..overflow)` before the loop; consider a `(dh,n)`-keyed map and/or a lower
  `MAX_SKIP_TOTAL`.

### L2 — Sealed-sender metadata has no forward secrecy

- **Location:** `crates/logos-sealed/src/lib.rs:130-171` (`seal`, encrypts to
  `recipient_identity_dh_pub` at `:138`) and `:174-210` (`unseal` at `:182`).
- **Impact:** The outer sealed-sender envelope is an anonymous sealed box: a fresh
  ephemeral sender key DH'd against the recipient's **long-term static identity DH
  key**, which never rotates. The `SealedContent` (server-signed sender cert =
  sender_username + sender_identity) has no per-message ratchet and no recipient-side
  ephemeral. The layer is classical X25519 only (no ML-KEM).
- **Attack:** The untrusted relay archives every opaque envelope it routes. Later
  compromise of a recipient's long-term identity DH private key (device seizure/backup
  theft, or future X25519 cryptanalysis — harvest-now-decrypt-later) lets the attacker
  replay archived envelopes through `unseal()` and recover the sender cert for every
  message ever delivered, reconstructing the historical social graph. The inner ratchet
  payload stays protected; only the sender-identity metadata is retroactively exposed.
- **Verification note:** Split refuted/confirmed. The deanonymization requires
  compromise of the recipient's long-term key (the relay alone cannot do it), and the
  threat model only promises *classical* sealed-sender metadata — so the PQ harvest-now
  angle is an acknowledged limitation. This matches Signal's accepted sealed-sender
  property. Genuine FS gap, but conditional; Low.
- **Fix:** Document explicitly as a known limitation. For real metadata FS, derive the
  envelope key from an ephemeral-to-**rotating** recipient sealed-sender prekey (signed,
  short-lived) instead of the long-term identity DH key, and delete expired prekey
  secrets so a future identity-key compromise can't decrypt past envelopes.

### L3 — Unzeroized transient copies of the long-term identity secret

Merged: `from_secret_bytes` (crypto-primitives) and the recovery-path buffers
(recovery-identity).

- **Locations:** `crates/logos-identity/src/lib.rs:107-120` (`from_secret_bytes` builds
  `SigningKey`/`X25519Secret` from local `ed`/`dh` arrays and returns without zeroizing
  — unlike the sibling `from_seed` at `:137-138`); `crates/logos-client/src/lib.rs:
  281-309` (`one_chunk`/`phrase_to_secret`), `:344-368` (`restore`), `:373-386`
  (`export_recovery_phrase`) — the master seed / 64-byte secret / `to_entropy()` Vec
  are left in freed memory; `bip39` is pulled without its `zeroize` feature.
- **Impact:** Defense-in-depth inconsistency. The struct derives `ZeroizeOnDrop` and
  `Store::drop` wipes the persistent secret, but these transient stack/heap copies of
  the same non-rotatable identity seed are not wiped. Recovering the seed once is a
  permanent full identity clone (and reconstructs the recovery phrase).
- **Verification note:** Verifiers split low/info and judged it **not independently
  exploitable**: the identical secret already lives durably in plaintext in
  `Store.identity_secret: Vec<u8>` for the whole session, and `export_recovery_phrase`
  returns the phrase as a plaintext String to Swift by design — so zeroizing the
  transients closes no real path while those persist. Hygiene item.
- **Fix:** Mirror `from_seed`: `ed.zeroize(); dh.zeroize();` before returning. Wrap the
  recovery-path `[u8;32]`/`[u8;64]` buffers and the `to_entropy()` Vec in `Zeroizing`,
  and enable bip39's zeroize feature.

### L4 — `StoreKey.swift` ignores Security-framework return codes

Merged: ignored `SecRandomCopyBytes` status and ignored `SecItemAdd`/`SecItemDelete`
status.

- **Locations:** `ios/LogosApp/Sources/StoreKey.swift:23-27` (RNG) and `:49-60`
  (Keychain `save`).
- **Impact:**
  - `raw()` zero-fills a 32-byte buffer, calls `SecRandomCopyBytes`, and discards the
    `OSStatus` (`_ = ...`). On the (rare) failure path the buffer stays all-zero and is
    persisted as the permanent at-rest master key — root of both the Argon2id store
    password (`base64(raw)`) and the ChaCha20-Poly1305 history seal — making store and
    history trivially decryptable offline.
  - `save()` discards both `SecItemDelete` and `SecItemAdd` statuses. If `SecItemAdd`
    fails, the freshly minted key is never persisted but `raw()` still returns it; the
    session works, then the next launch mints a new key, the encrypted store fails to
    decrypt, and `loadHistory` overwrites the unreadable history — silent identity +
    history loss.
- **Verification note:** Both rated not exploitable: neither in-scope adversary (relay,
  peer) can induce an RNG or Keychain failure on the victim device, and
  `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly` is available during interactive
  onboarding, so the cited specific failure modes are implausible. Both are silent,
  catastrophic-if-triggered error-handling gaps. Low.
- **Fix:** `guard SecRandomCopyBytes(...) == errSecSuccess else { fatalError(...) }`;
  capture `SecItemAdd`'s status and refuse to return/use a key that wasn't persisted
  (propagate an error so onboarding can retry/abort). Never `save()`/encrypt under an
  unverified key buffer.

### L5 — `save()` failure silently drops the just-decrypted batch

- **Location:** `crates/logos-client/src/lib.rs:732-773` (recv loop + `save()` at
  `:753`) with `establish_and_decrypt :998-1012` and Normal-decrypt `:814-826`.
- **Impact:** `recv()` mutates `self.store` in place per envelope (inserts session,
  removes consumed prekeys, advances the ratchet) and builds `out` before the single
  `save()`. If `save()` errs, `recv()` returns `Err` and the caller never gets `out`,
  but the in-memory store has already advanced. On the next `recv()` against the same
  long-lived `Client` (iOS keeps one for the app lifetime), the prekey envelope is seen
  as a duplicate (`Ok(None)` → ACK/delete) and Normal envelopes fail against the
  advanced ratchet (`Ok(None)` → ACK/delete) — messages deleted from the relay without
  ever being shown. The durability comment at `:750-752` only holds across a process
  restart (stale on-disk reload), not within one `Client`.
- **Verification note:** Not adversarially triggerable (the relay can't force a local
  `save()` failure given the body cap); realistic trigger is local ENOSPC or iOS
  FileProtection write-denial while locked. Availability/data-integrity, Low.
- **Fix:** Snapshot the store and roll back in-memory mutations if `save()` fails, or
  process+save one envelope at a time so a failure forfeits only the single unsaved
  message; never count a message delivered until its advancing state is durable.

### L6 — Snapshot has no fsync and a corrupt snapshot is fatal on boot

- **Location:** `crates/logos-server/src/lib.rs:249-252` (write+rename, no fsync),
  `:255-270` / `:186-190` (`load_snapshot` fatal on parse error).
- **Impact:** `save_snapshot` writes the tmp file and renames without fsyncing the file
  or directory, so a power loss can durably surface the rename with truncated contents.
  `load_snapshot` propagates any `serde_json` parse error as a fatal startup error, so a
  damaged `snapshot.json` prevents the relay from booting at all — taking down the whole
  directory + all queued mailboxes.
- **Verification note:** Both verifiers confirm the code but judge it **not
  exploitable**: corruption requires power loss / FS damage, not any in-scope
  adversary's capability (the snapshot round-trips validated in-memory state, and
  disk-full errors before rename leave the prior file intact). Availability/durability,
  Low. Operator recovery is trivial (delete the file).
- **Fix:** fsync the tmp file before rename and fsync the parent dir after; on load,
  treat a corrupt snapshot as recoverable (log, move aside, start empty / load
  last-known-good) instead of aborting startup.

### L7 — CI installs build tools unpinned

- **Location:** `.github/workflows/ios.yml:57` (`cargo install cargo-audit --locked`),
  `.github/workflows/ios-release.yml:81` and `.github/workflows/ios.yml:79`
  (`brew install xcodegen`). Related: `ios.yml:55-58` vs `deny.toml:19-23` advisory-
  triage divergence (no `.cargo/audit.toml`).
- **Impact:** Every `uses:` action is SHA-pinned, but these two tools are fetched at
  latest each run (`--locked` only honors cargo-audit's own bundled lockfile, not its
  version). The xcodegen install runs in the same release job that later writes the
  decoded ASC signing key (`AuthKey_*.p8`) to disk. A compromised xcodegen Homebrew
  formula/bottle could plant persistence (PATH shim, modified `xcodebuild`) that reads
  the on-disk ASC key later in the job and exfiltrates it, enabling malicious signed
  builds. A compromised cargo-audit release runs arbitrary code in the (constrained,
  no-Apple-secrets) Linux job.
- **Verification note:** Split refuted/confirmed — refuted on the basis that this needs
  an out-of-scope third-party (RustSec/crates.io/Homebrew) compromise, confirmed as a
  real CI-hardening outlier with a plausible signing-key-theft path. The deny.toml
  divergence is benign today (unmaintained-kind advisories, `cargo audit` exits 0) but
  is a process inconsistency (Info).
- **Fix:** Pin cargo-audit to an exact version (`--version X.Y.Z`) or use a checksum-
  verified binary / official advisory action. Pin xcodegen to an exact formula version
  or a checksum-verified tarball, and install it in a separate prep job whose output is
  consumed by the signing job so the tool install never shares a job with the decoded
  ASC key. Add a single advisory-triage source of truth (drop the standalone `cargo
  audit` and rely on `cargo deny check advisories`, or add `.cargo/audit.toml` with the
  same ignore IDs).

---

### I1 — Non-strict ed25519 verify + no low-order X25519 validation (crypto hardening, not exploitable)

- **Locations:** `crates/logos-identity/src/lib.rs:162` and `crates/logos-sealed/src/
  lib.rs:95` use permissive `vk.verify()` rather than `verify_strict`;
  `crates/logos-pqxdh/src/lib.rs:62-66,108-110,166-168` call X25519 `diffie_hellman`
  on attacker-supplied points with no low-order/all-zero check.
- **Why Info / not exploitable:** ed25519-dalek 2.x already rejects non-canonical S
  (no S-malleability); the residual cofactor/mixed-order acceptance matters only if a
  signature later becomes a uniqueness/cross-identity-binding primitive (e.g. key
  transparency) — every current verify site already trusts/pins the signer (TOFU,
  server key, or self-asserted PoP). For X25519, a party able to send a low-order
  handshake already knows the resulting root key, cannot impersonate a pinned contact
  (transcript binds both identities; a real contact's key isn't low-order), and the
  relay's documented first-contact substitution already achieves the equivalent.
  Genuine defense-in-depth.
- **Fix:** Switch all identity-signature checks to `verify_strict`. After each X25519
  `diffie_hellman`, reject all-zero / non-contributory results (or pre-validate inputs
  against the small-order set) and abort the handshake.

### I2 — Normal path doesn't cross-check `cert.sender_identity` vs pinned peer (not exploitable)

- **Location:** `crates/logos-client/src/lib.rs:814-831` (Normal handling looks up the
  session by `cert.sender_username` only), vs the explicit bind at `:941` on the prekey
  path.
- **Why Info / not exploitable:** The Normal ratchet message is AEAD-authenticated and
  only decrypts under the genuine pinned peer's chain keys; a forged/mismatched cert
  yields a decrypt failure (harmless ACK-drop), and `cert.sender_identity` is never used
  for lookup/display/re-pin on this path. The cert is still signature/expiry-verified in
  `unseal()`. A defense-in-depth asymmetry, not a vulnerability — would only matter if
  ratchet authenticity were weakened or sessions re-keyed.
- **Fix:** For uniformity, after locating the session assert
  `cert.sender_identity == session.peer_identity` and drop on mismatch, so both message
  paths bind the cert to the cryptographically-authenticated peer.

---

## Residual Risk / NOT Covered

- **Cryptographic primitive correctness in depth.** This review reasoned about protocol
  composition and call sites; it did not perform formal verification, constant-time
  analysis of the dalek/ML-KEM dependencies, or differential testing of the ratchet/
  PQXDH against the Signal reference. The KDF/transcript constructions were read, not
  proven.
- **Key transparency.** There is no key-transparency mechanism yet; first-contact
  identity substitution by the untrusted relay is accepted under TOFU. This is a known
  design state, not a bug, but it is the largest standing trust assumption.
- **Fully compromised / unlocked device** is partly out of scope per the threat model;
  several memory-hygiene items (L3) are only meaningful against that boundary and were
  rated accordingly.
- **Network MITM** against the pinned-cert relay is largely out of scope and was not
  fuzzed; TLS/pinning configuration on the Swift side was not exercised at runtime.
- **Supply chain beyond CI tool pinning** (full transitive dependency review, build
  reproducibility, signing-pipeline secret handling beyond the noted job-isolation gap)
  was only spot-checked.
- **iOS app surface** beyond the audited identity-teardown/poll/persist paths
  (UI state machines, deep links, pasteboard, background tasks, biometric gating) was
  not comprehensively reviewed.
- **DoS quantification.** The HIGH relay-DoS findings were confirmed structurally and
  by code reasoning; exact throughput-collapse thresholds were not load-tested.
- **No runtime exploitation** was performed; findings are from static review of the
  actual source plus the verifiers' code tracing. No PoCs were executed against a live
  relay or device.

## Disclaimer

This is an **internal AI-driven red-team assessment**. It is intended to surface and
prioritize plausible issues quickly, not to certify the system. It does **not** replace
a professional third-party cryptographic audit, and Logos is explicitly experimental.
Before relying on Logos for any sensitive use, commission an independent review by
qualified human cryptographers and security engineers — particularly of the PQXDH/
ratchet composition, the sealed-sender metadata guarantees, and the relay's abuse
resistance.
